subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Taylor / Maclaurin Series
stimulus:
The function $g$ has derivatives of all orders for all real numbers. The Maclaurin series for $g$ is given by
$$g(x) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n x^n}{2e^n + 3}.$$

parts.prompt: State the conditions necessary to apply the integral test to the series $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{e^n}$. Then use the integral test to show that $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{e^n}$ converges.
parts.scoring_notes: The integral test requires the associated function $f(x) = e^{-x}$ to be positive, continuous, and decreasing on $[0, \infty)$. The improper integral must be set up and evaluated correctly.

scoring_points.description: States all three necessary conditions for the integral test: the function $f(x) = \frac{1}{e^x} = e^{-x}$ must be (1) positive, (2) continuous, and (3) decreasing on $[0, \infty)$ (or $[1, \infty)$). All three conditions are satisfied: $e^{-x} > 0$ for all $x$, $e^{-x}$ is continuous everywhere, and $e^{-x}$ is strictly decreasing.

required_elements: All three conditions stated: positive, continuous, and decreasing on $[0,\infty)$

scoring_points.description: Sets up the correct improper integral: $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} e^{-x}\,dx = \lim_{b \to \infty} \int_0^b e^{-x}\,dx$

required_elements: $\int_0^{\infty} e^{-x}\,dx$ or equivalent improper integral setup

scoring_points.description: Evaluates the improper integral and concludes convergence: $\displaystyle\lim_{b \to \infty}\left[-e^{-x}\right]_0^b = \lim_{b \to \infty}\left(-e^{-b} + e^0\right) = 0 + 1 = 1$. Since the integral converges (equals $1$), the series $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{e^n}$ converges by the integral test.

required_elements: Concludes series converges by integral test

parts.prompt: Use the limit comparison test with the series $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{e^n}$ to show that the series $g(1) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n}{2e^n + 3}$ converges absolutely.
parts.scoring_notes: To show absolute convergence, the student must consider $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2e^n + 3}$ and apply the limit comparison test with $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{e^n}$.

scoring_points.description: Sets up and computes the limit comparison: let $a_n = \dfrac{1}{2e^n + 3}$ and $b_n = \dfrac{1}{e^n}$. Computes $\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{a_n}{b_n} = \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{e^n}{2e^n + 3} = \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{1}{2 + 3e^{-n}} = \frac{1}{2}$.

Conclusion: since the limit $\frac{1}{2}$ is positive and finite, and $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{e^n}$ converges (shown in part (a)), by the limit comparison test $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2e^n + 3}$ converges. Therefore $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n}{2e^n + 3}$ converges absolutely.

parts.prompt: Determine the radius of convergence of the Maclaurin series $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n x^n}{2e^n + 3}$ for $g$. Show the work that leads to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Use the ratio test. The radius of convergence is $R = e$.

scoring_points.description: Sets up the ratio test correctly: $\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\left|\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right| = \lim_{n\to\infty}\left|\frac{(-1)^{n+1}x^{n+1}}{2e^{n+1}+3} \cdot \frac{2e^n+3}{(-1)^n x^n}\right| = \lim_{n\to\infty} |x|\cdot\frac{2e^n+3}{2e^{n+1}+3}$

Evaluates the limit of the ratio: $\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{2e^n + 3}{2e^{n+1}+3} = \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{2e^n+3}{2e\cdot e^n+3} = \frac{2}{2e} = \frac{1}{e}$. So the ratio test limit equals $\dfrac{|x|}{e}$.
Sets the ratio test limit less than $1$ and determines the radius of convergence: $\dfrac{|x|}{e} < 1 \Rightarrow |x| < e$. Therefore the radius of convergence is $R = e$.

parts.prompt: The first two terms of the series $g(1) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n}{2e^n + 3}$ are used to approximate $g(1)$. Use the alternating series error bound to determine an upper bound for the error of this approximation.
parts.scoring_notes: The alternating series error bound equals the absolute value of the first omitted term, which is the $n=2$ term.

scoring_points.description: Identifies the correct error bound as the absolute value of the third term ($n = 2$): the first two terms used are $n = 0$ and $n = 1$, so the first omitted term is $\dfrac{(-1)^2}{2e^2+3} = \dfrac{1}{2e^2+3}$. By the alternating series error bound, the error is at most $\dfrac{1}{2e^2 + 3}$.
